articles.title: Scientists Find Old Life in a Surprising Place
articles.summary: Scientists were hiking in Morocco when they saw something strange in rocks. They found tiny ridges called wrinkle structures, which are usually made by algae in shallow water where sunlight reaches. But these wrinkles were in deep water rocks, too dark for algae to live. This means they might have been made by different tiny life forms that don't need sunlight. The rocks are about 180 million years old, and such wrinkles are rare because animals often destroy them. The scientists tested the rocks and found clues that show these wrinkles came from living things. This discovery helps us learn about ancient life in the deep ocean.

articles.title: Eat More Fiber for a Healthy Gut
articles.summary: Eating fiber is very good for your health. Fiber helps your stomach work well and can keep you from getting sick. A new idea called fibermaxxing means eating enough fiber every day. A scientist named Jennifer Lee says this is popular because people want to stay healthy as they get older. She explains that not eating enough fiber can lead to problems like gaining weight or getting diseases like diabetes. Fiber comes from foods like fruits, vegetables, and whole grains. The USDA says adults should eat between 22 and 34 grams of fiber each day, depending on how old they are and if they are a boy or girl. Eating more fiber can make you feel better and help you live a healthier life.

articles.title: A Tiny Dinosaur with Long Arms Changes What We Know About Small Dinosaurs
articles.summary: Scientists found a new tiny dinosaur called Alnashetri that lived 90 million years ago in Argentina. It was very small, weighing only 700 grams, and had long arms, unlike other small dinosaurs in its group called Alvarezsaurids. Before, people thought these dinosaurs got small to eat ants and termites, but Alnashetri probably hunted insects and small mammals instead. This discovery shows that dinosaurs didn't always get smaller in a straight line for one reason. It helps us learn that evolution can be more complicated, with body sizes changing in different ways. The fossil is important because it's the most complete and smallest of its kind found in South America.

articles.title: Satellites Help Find Weak Bridges
articles.summary: Scientists are using satellites from space to find bridges that might be weak or in danger of breaking. They looked at 744 bridges all over the world and found that bridges in North America are in the worst condition. Many bridges were built a long time ago and are getting old. Satellites can take pictures of bridges to see tiny movements, as small as a few millimeters, that might mean a bridge is starting to fail. This helps catch problems early before a bridge collapses. The satellites use a special tool called Synthetic Aperture Radar to watch bridges from space. This is important because it can check bridges more often and in places where it's hard to send people. By using satellites, we can make bridges safer for everyone.

articles.title: Saving Special Water Streams in England
articles.summary: People who care about nature want to protect chalk streams better. Chalk streams are rare freshwater habitats found mostly in England. They have clear water and gravel bottoms because they come from springs in chalky ground. There are only 220 chalk streams in the world, and most are in southern England, like the River Wandle in London. The London Wildlife Trust says these streams need more legal protection to keep them safe from harm. They want the government to include chalk streams in rules that help plan how land is used. The government says it is working to clean up water and invest money to fix chalk streams. Water companies are also making plans to improve these streams and reduce pollution.

articles.title: Helping Animals with Old Hedge Tricks
articles.summary: Bristol Water is using an old method called hedge laying to help animals and birds near Blagdon Lake in Somerset. Hedge laying is a craft where people trim hedges and lay the stems sideways to make them thick and bushy. This gives animals like birds and hedgehogs safe places to live and hide, especially in winter. The project will take two years and stop during nesting time so birds are not bothered. Experts like Mike Reed are teaching this skill to others. They hope many animals will come back to the area soon.
